evidence: Many tiny config snapshot types/functions exist as independent pass-through wrappers rather than a consolidated domain API (`goseg/config/config.go:279-377`).

Callers in multiple subsystems repeatedly compose runtime behavior through nested function-seam structs (`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`).

Several runtime paths create new runtime objects repeatedly only to forward through additional wrapper layers without semantic transformation (`goseg/routines/docker.go:88-103`, `goseg/routines/docker.go:180-219`, `goseg/broadcast/loop.go:86-151`).

findings.summary: Config is exposed through many narrow snapshot structs that duplicate a copy-on-read pattern, creating low-leverage abstraction debt across package boundaries.
evidence: Each feature area defines independent snapshot views over `Conf()` (`goseg/config/config.go:279-377`).

Collector and routine layers create helper shims that exist solely to wrap those snapshots without domain logic (`goseg/broadcast/collectors/collectors.go:32-38`, `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go:44-48`, `89-93`).
Higher-level orchestration code directly depends on many snapshot functions (`goseg/importer/importer.go:69-78`, `goseg/main.go:92-104`) instead of a bounded config contract.
findings.suggestion: Replace the ad-hoc snapshot family with bounded read interfaces (for example `ShipConfigView`, `RuntimeConfigView`, `AuthConfigView`) and inject explicit dependencies at subsystem boundaries, so each caller can request only the small set of fields it truly needs with clear ownership and stronger API semantics.

findings.summary: Runtime-seam layers and helper wrappers are stacked broadly for testability but add little domain behavior and increase trace complexity.
evidence: Broadcast exposes a custom runtime struct with many function fields plus defaulting logic, yet execution remains a thin pass-through sequence (`goseg/broadcast/loop.go:13-40`, `49-83`).
Docker routines mirror the same style with multiple nested seam structs and direct forwarding (`goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go:24-65`, `84-103`).
Update/event-handling paths instantiate runtime wrappers and forward through wrapper methods rather than domain transitions (`goseg/routines/docker.go:88-103`, `180-219`, `227-260`).
findings.suggestion: Constrain seams to true boundaries only (one seam per subsystem boundary), and replace pass-through function buckets with direct interfaces that carry one clear behavioral contract; avoid per-event/per-call wrapper reconstruction for hot paths.

findings.summary: Default no-op seam implementations in orchestration/runtime construction obscure whether required dependencies are actually configured.
evidence: The base docker runtime sets wireguard/minio/netdata operations to inert defaults that return success-like placeholders (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go:146-157`).
Load paths branch on optional writer hooks without explicit invariant enforcement, making feature readiness implicit (`goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go:34-38`, `213-229`).
Lifecycle uses mutable package-level function variables and setters for dependency overrides without a contract enforcing completeness (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:20-31`, `47-63`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit `runtime.RuntimeDependencies` interfaces for orchestration and lifecycle, fail-fast on missing implementations in constructors, and remove silent no-op defaults unless semantically optional.
